name: Abydos Helicopter
description: The Abydos Helicopter refers to a set of alleged hieroglyphic carvings found in the Temple of Seti I at Abydos, Egypt. Proponents of alternative history interpret these carvings as depicting modern vehicles such as a helicopter, a submarine, and a jet plane, suggesting advanced technology existed in ancient Egypt, possibly influenced by extraterrestrial contact or a lost civilization. The carvings appear within a cartouche (an oval enclosure containing royal names) and are said to be the result of palimpsest, where the original cartouche was plastered over and re-carved during a later pharaoh's reign, resulting in overlapping images. Mainstream Egyptologists dismiss this interpretation, arguing that the 'helicopter' and other supposed vehicles are simply the result of erosion, overlapping cartouches, and pareidolia, the human tendency to see familiar patterns in random stimuli. They assert that the original hieroglyphs were standard royal titles and epithets, and the apparent resemblance to modern technology is coincidental, arising from the specific combination of eroded and superimposed symbols. The theory gained popularity through internet forums, documentaries, and books promoting alternative historical interpretations.
subtitle: Ancient hieroglyphs or modern machines?

claims.claim: The Abydos hieroglyphs depict modern aircraft and vehicles.
claims.evidence: The shapes of the carvings resemble a helicopter, submarine, and jet.
claims.criticism: Egyptologists attribute the shapes to erosion, overlapping cartouches, and pareidolia.

claims.claim: Ancient Egyptians possessed advanced technology or were influenced by extraterrestrial beings.
claims.evidence: The presence of 'modern' vehicles suggests knowledge beyond their supposed capabilities.
claims.criticism: There is no other archaeological evidence supporting advanced technology or extraterrestrial contact in ancient Egypt. The interpretation relies solely on the ambiguous carvings.

claims.claim: The overlapping cartouches were deliberately altered to conceal advanced knowledge.
claims.evidence: The deliberate re-carving suggests a hidden message or attempt to erase history.
claims.criticism: Overlapping cartouches were a common practice when a new pharaoh replaced the previous one, updating the royal names and titles. There is no evidence of a deliberate attempt to conceal knowledge.

keyFigures.name: Erich von Däniken
keyFigures.role: Popularized the idea of ancient astronauts, which influenced the interpretation of the Abydos carvings.

keyFigures.name: Frank Yurco
keyFigures.role: Egyptologist who debunked the theory, explaining the overlapping cartouches and erosion.

keyFigures.name: David Childress
keyFigures.role: Promoter of alternative history and ancient technology, featured the Abydos helicopter in his books and documentaries.

mythologyConnections: Osiris myth (Abydos as a sacred site)
Royal cartouches and their symbolic power

timeline.event: Temple of Seti I at Abydos is discovered and documented.
Alternative history proponents begin to interpret the carvings as modern vehicles.
The 'Abydos Helicopter' theory gains popularity online and in alternative media.

sources.author: David Childress
sources.work: Technology of the Gods: The Incredible Sciences of the Ancients
sources.title: David Childress

sources.author: Frank Yurco
sources.work: Response to claims of 'modern' technology in Abydos hieroglyphs (unpublished)
sources.title: Frank Yurco
